questions.passage:
The surviving contracts of the Italian fifteenth century make disconcerting reading for anyone raised on the modern idea of the artist. A patron commissioning an altarpiece did not ask a painter to express himself. He negotiated a legal instrument, drawn up by a notary, that specified the subject, the dimensions, the delivery date, the penalties for lateness, and, with particular care, the materials. Ultramarine, ground from lapis lazuli imported through Venice, was priced by grade, and a contract might stipulate that the mantle of the Virgin be painted in ultramarine at two florins the ounce while lesser figures received the cheaper azurite. Gold for the ground and the halos was itemized in the same spirit. The painter in these documents is not a genius awaiting inspiration; he is a contractor supplying skilled labor and accountable, like a mason, for his materials and his schedule.

The contracts also reveal what patrons feared. Workshops were collaborative enterprises in which assistants painted drapery, backgrounds, and minor saints, and a client paying a master's prices wanted assurance that he was getting the master. Hence the recurring clause requiring that the painting be executed by the master's own hand, or at minimum that he paint the principal figures himself. Such language would be unintelligible if buyers had thought of paintings as interchangeable devotional furniture. It shows, on the contrary, a market that had learned to distinguish hands, and to pay differently for them.

Over the second half of the century the emphasis of these documents shifts in a way historians have found revealing. Specifications of pigment grade and gold weight become rarer, while clauses about the master's hand, and about skill more generally, multiply. A discerning patron of the 1480s advertised his taste not by the weight of precious material on his walls but by his ability to recognize difficult foreshortening, convincing gesture, and a well-composed narrative. Skill had become the precious pigment. The change did not make painting less commercial; it changed what the commerce was in. Ability, unlike lapis lazuli, could not be weighed on a scale, so buyers needed connoisseurship, intermediaries, and reputation to price it, and painters needed recognizable manners to be priced at all. Individual style, in other words, first flourished not as a rebellion against the market but as a product the market had learned to demand.

It was only later that this artisanal world acquired its familiar costume. Vasari, writing in the middle of the sixteenth century and campaigning to raise painting from the mechanical to the liberal arts, recast the contractors of the Quattrocento as heroes of individual destiny. His Lives supplied childhood omens, divine gifts, and rivalries of temperament where the archives record delivery dates and disputed payments. The book was a brilliant piece of professional advocacy, and it succeeded so completely that its categories still govern the popular understanding of the period. Museum labels tell us that a master broke free of convention; the notarial record tells us that he fulfilled clause three of a contract witnessed by two wool merchants.

None of this diminishes the paintings. The altarpieces of the period are what they are whether we imagine their makers as vessels of genius or as businessmen with exceptional hands. But the two pictures of the painter support very different histories. If we take the contracts seriously, the interesting story of the fifteenth century is not the liberation of the artist from commerce but the education of commerce itself: the slow training of buyers to see, and to pay for, qualities that no scale could measure. The solitary genius, so far as the documents show, arrived afterward, wearing clothes the market had already tailored.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Vasari's Lives is so unreliable a source that historians of fifteenth-century painting should set it aside entirely in favor of the surviving notarial records.
choices.B: Fifteenth-century patrons valued precious materials above artistic skill until Vasari taught them to prize individual genius.
choices.C: Renaissance painting operated as contractual commerce, and individual style first gained its value within that market rather than in opposition to it.
choices.D: The collaborative structure of Renaissance workshops made it impossible for patrons to know which painter had executed their commissions.

explanations.A: The passage calls Vasari's book brilliant advocacy and uses it to explain how the period was reimagined; it never recommends discarding it as a source.
explanations.B: The shift from pigment clauses to skill clauses occurred within the fifteenth century itself, decades before Vasari wrote, so the change in taste cannot be credited to him.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage moves from contracts that treat painters as accountable contractors to the argument that recognizable individual style 'first flourished not as a rebellion against the market but as a product the market had learned to demand.'
explanations.D: The own-hand clauses show patrons actively securing the master's participation, which the passage presents as evidence that buyers could and did distinguish hands.

questions.stem: In the context of the passage, the statement that 'skill had become the precious pigment' most nearly means that:

choices.A: painters of the 1480s began to grind rarer and more expensive materials than earlier workshops had used.
choices.B: the price of ultramarine rose so steeply that patrons substituted the master's labor for costly blue.
choices.C: connoisseurs came to believe that technical ability was a divine endowment beyond monetary valuation.
choices.D: demonstrable mastery replaced costly materials as the scarce quality patrons paid a premium to display.

explanations.A: The sentence marks a movement away from material expenditure, not an escalation of it; pigment specifications were becoming rarer in contracts at just this time.
explanations.B: The passage attributes the shift to changing taste among discerning patrons, not to any change in the cost of lapis lazuli, which it never mentions.
explanations.C: The metaphor keeps skill firmly inside the world of pricing; the passage immediately discusses how buyers used connoisseurship and reputation to put a value on ability.
explanations.D: Correct. The surrounding sentences explain that a patron of the 1480s advertised his taste through recognition of foreshortening and composition rather than through the weight of gold and lapis, so skill inherited the role costly pigment had played as the prized, scarce commodity.

questions.stem: The paragraph discussing Vasari serves primarily to:

choices.A: identify the moment when painting genuinely ceased to be a commercial activity.
choices.B: concede that the surviving documentary record of the Quattrocento is far too thin and fragmentary to support firm conclusions about painters.
choices.C: explain how the commercial reality preserved in the archives came to be overwritten by a heroic narrative of individual genius.
choices.D: prove that sixteenth-century writers had no access to the contracts historians now study.

explanations.A: The passage never claims painting stopped being commercial; Vasari's contribution is described as a change in costume and category, not in economic reality.
explanations.B: The author treats the archives as rich and legible, contrasting delivery dates and disputed payments with Vasari's omens and rivalries; no concession about thin evidence appears.
explanations.C: Correct. The paragraph presents the Lives as professional advocacy that 'recast the contractors of the Quattrocento as heroes of individual destiny' and succeeded so completely that its categories still shape museum labels, accounting for the gap between popular understanding and the notarial record.
explanations.D: The passage says nothing about what documents Vasari could consult; his recasting is explained by his campaign to elevate painting, not by ignorance of contracts.

questions.stem: Based on the passage, a patron in the 1450s who paid the highest available rates for an altarpiece most likely expected:

choices.A: a painting whose subject matter departed from established devotional convention.
choices.B: top-grade materials in the principal figures and the master's own hand in the most important parts of the work.
choices.C: a composition demonstrating difficult foreshortening, convincing narrative gesture, and well-managed pictorial storytelling above all else.
choices.D: exclusive rights to reproduce the finished image in other churches he endowed.

explanations.A: The contracts described for this period fix the subject in advance as part of the legal instrument, so a high price bought quality of execution, not novelty of subject.
explanations.B: Correct. Mid-century contracts, as the passage describes them, priced ultramarine by grade for the most important figures and used own-hand clauses to guarantee the master's participation, so a top-paying patron would expect precisely those two assurances.
explanations.C: Taste centered on foreshortening and narrative skill is what the passage assigns to the discerning patron of the 1480s, after the documentary emphasis had shifted away from materials.
explanations.D: Reproduction rights appear nowhere in the passage's account of what contracts specified, which concerned subject, size, schedule, materials, and hands.

questions.stem: Which of the following archival discoveries would most strengthen the author's account of the late fifteenth-century shift in patronage?

choices.A: Letters showing that Vasari privately doubted several anecdotes he included in the Lives.
choices.B: Evidence that Venetian import duties on lapis lazuli remained constant throughout the century.
choices.C: A treatise from the 1440s instructing prospective patrons to judge commissioned altarpieces chiefly by the weight and purity of their gold grounds.
choices.D: A large sample of contracts from the 1480s in which payment scales with the master's personal participation while pigment grades go unmentioned.

explanations.A: Vasari's private doubts would bear on his reliability as a biographer, not on the fifteenth-century contractual shift the author describes, which rests on notarial evidence.
explanations.B: Stable import duties are consistent with the author's account but do no work for it, since he attributes the shift to taste rather than to material costs.
explanations.C: A treatise praising gold weight in the 1440s matches the earlier regime the author already describes; it documents the starting point rather than the change.
explanations.D: Correct. The author's claim is that late-century documents multiply skill and own-hand clauses while material specifications recede; a large body of contracts tying payment to the master's participation and omitting pigment grades would be direct documentary confirmation of exactly that pattern.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes the author's position on the figure of the solitary artistic genius?

choices.A: It is an anachronism projected backward onto craftsmen whose working lives the documents show to have been commercial.
choices.B: It accurately describes a handful of exceptional masters while misrepresenting the ordinary workshop painter.
choices.C: It is an essentially harmless fiction, since the enduring quality of the paintings renders all questions of historical accuracy unimportant.
choices.D: It was invented by patrons seeking to inflate the resale value of works they had commissioned.

explanations.A: Correct. The closing image of the genius arriving afterward 'wearing clothes the market had already tailored,' together with the contrast between museum labels and clause three of a witnessed contract, treats the figure as a later construction imposed on artisanal businessmen.
explanations.B: The passage draws no line between exceptional masters and ordinary painters; even the most sought-after hands appear in the documents as contractors bound by schedules and clauses.
explanations.C: Although the author says the myth does not diminish the paintings, he insists the two pictures of the painter 'support very different histories,' so the fiction is consequential rather than harmless.
explanations.D: The passage credits the heroic recasting to Vasari's professional advocacy on behalf of painters, not to patrons or to resale considerations, which it never raises.
